questions.text: Who is the 'Little Girl' in the story?
questions.explanation: Kezia is the central character, referred to as the little girl who is afraid of her father.

questions.text: How did Kezia feel about her father at the beginning of the story?
options: She was afraid of him and avoided him
questions.explanation: To the little girl, he was a figure to be feared and avoided.

questions.text: What was Kezia's father's routine before going to his office?
options: He would give her a casual kiss
He would have breakfast with her
questions.explanation: Every morning before going to work he came into her room and gave her a casual kiss.

questions.text: What was the sound Kezia heard with a sense of relief?
questions.explanation: She felt relieved when the sound of the carriage grew fainter as her father left for work.

questions.text: Kezia's father's hands and neck appeared ________ to her.
questions.explanation: Thinking about him alone was like thinking about a giant because of his large size.

questions.text: What would Kezia do when talking to her father?
questions.explanation: She never stuttered with other people but only with her father because she was trying so hard to say words properly.

questions.text: Who suggested Kezia make a gift for her father's birthday?
questions.explanation: Her grandmother suggested she make a pin-cushion for his birthday.

questions.text: What did Kezia use to stuff the pin-cushion?
options: Scraps of paper from her father's table
questions.explanation: She found some fine paper on the bed-table, tore them into pieces, and stuffed her case.

questions.text: What were the papers Kezia tore up?
options: A great speech for the Port Authority
questions.explanation: The papers were her father's important speech for the Port Authority.

questions.text: How did the father punish Kezia for tearing the papers?
options: He locked her in a room
He beat her with a ruler
questions.explanation: He hit her pink little palms with a ruler to teach her not to touch what does not belong to her.

questions.text: Who were the neighbors of Kezia's family?
questions.explanation: The Macdonalds lived next door and had five children.

questions.text: What did Kezia see the Macdonalds doing in their garden?
questions.explanation: She saw the father playing happily with his children, which made her think there were 'different sorts of fathers'.

questions.text: Why did Kezia have to sleep alone one night?
options: Her mother and grandmother were at the hospital
questions.explanation: Her mother fell ill and her grandmother went with her to the hospital.

questions.text: Who was Alice in the story?
questions.explanation: Alice was the cook who looked after Kezia during the day.

questions.text: What was the nightmare Kezia often had?
options: A butcher with a knife and a rope
questions.explanation: She dreamt of a butcher with a knife and a rope, who came closer and closer with a dreadful smile.

questions.text: When Kezia woke up screaming from her nightmare, who was standing by her bed?
questions.explanation: Her father was standing by her bed with a candle in his hand.

questions.text: What did the father do when he found Kezia afraid after the nightmare?
options: He carried her to his big bedroom and tucked her in
questions.explanation: He showed his caring side by taking her to his room and comforting her.

questions.text: Kezia realized her father wasn't a giant but a man with a ________.
questions.explanation: She felt his heart beating and realized he had a big heart but was just too tired to be like Mr. Macdonald.

questions.text: Why did Kezia's father not play like Mr. Macdonald?
options: He was too tired after working all day
questions.explanation: He worked hard all day and was 'too tired to be a Mr. Macdonald'.

questions.text: What does the story 'The Little Girl' teach us?
options: Parents always hide their love behind discipline
questions.explanation: The story shows the transformation of Kezia's understanding of her father's love and character.

questions.text: On Sunday afternoons, where did Grandmother send Kezia?
options: To the drawing room to talk to parents
questions.explanation: Grandmother wanted Kezia to have a 'nice talk' with her father and mother.

questions.text: What was the father usually doing when Kezia went to the drawing room on Sundays?
options: Sleeping on the sofa with a handkerchief on his face
questions.explanation: He was usually stretched out on the sofa, snoring and sleeping soundly.

questions.text: The father compared Kezia to which animal when she stared at him?
questions.explanation: He would say, 'Don’t stare so, Kezia. You look like a little brown owl.'

questions.text: What color was the silk for the pin-cushion?
questions.explanation: She labored to stitch three sides of the cushion with yellow silk.

questions.text: Who said, 'What’s that for?' when Kezia was seen near the table?
questions.explanation: The father asked this when he was searching for his missing papers.

questions.text: What is 'stuttering'?
questions.explanation: Kezia struggled to speak clearly in front of her father due to fear.

questions.text: The name of the author of 'The Little Girl' is:
questions.explanation: Katherine Mansfield wrote this poignant story about family dynamics.

questions.text: When the father was looking for his papers, the house was full of ________.
questions.explanation: There was a lot of noise and searching (hue and cry) for the Port Authority speech.

questions.text: Kezia's grandmother was ________ towards her.
questions.explanation: She tried to bridge the gap between Kezia and her parents.

questions.text: Where was the 'Little Girl' hiding when the father came with the ruler?
questions.explanation: She hid under the bedsheets, but he pulled them aside.

questions.text: What was the father carrying when he took Kezia to his room?
questions.explanation: He held a candle to see the way to the bedroom.

questions.text: What did the father do to his newspaper before sleeping in the drawing room?
questions.explanation: It was part of his relaxation routine on Sunday afternoons.

questions.text: Kezia felt her father's 'hardness' was ________.
questions.explanation: She realized it was a 'nice hardness', different from the soft grandmother.

questions.text: How many children did the Macdonalds have?
questions.explanation: The story mentions Mr. Macdonald playing with his five children.

questions.text: What did Kezia's mother usually do on Sundays?
questions.explanation: She was usually seen reading in the drawing room.

questions.text: What does 'wretched' mean in the sentence 'I’m so wretched'?
questions.explanation: Kezia felt miserable after being punished.

questions.text: Why did the grandmother wrap Kezia in a shawl?
options: To comfort her after the beating
questions.explanation: She rocked her in the chair to soothe her crying.

questions.text: The story takes place in which setting?
questions.explanation: The narrative focus is entirely on the dynamics within Kezia's home.

questions.text: What changed Kezia's perspective about her father?
options: He comforted her during a nightmare
questions.explanation: His physical presence and protection during her fear revealed his true nature.

questions.text: Kezia’s father was a ________ man.
questions.explanation: He was strict but his actions at the end show he was a responsible parent.
